patches.rationale: The current DOI resolves to Fukai & Yokoyama (2017) Neodymium isotope heterogeneity (EPSL) — a completely unrelated geochemistry paper. The correct paper is Terra-Nova F, Amit H, Hartmann GA, Trindade RIF, Pinheiro KJ (2017) Relating the South Atlantic Anomaly and geomagnetic flux patches. Phys Earth Planet Inter 266:39–53. DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002. This is a critical credibility fix — a reviewer clicking our key citation would find geochemistry, not geomagnetism.

patches.find: demonstrate that reversed-flux patches in MHD simulations reproduce SAA splitting — including the timing, geometry, and rate of separation.
patches.replace: demonstrate that reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary drive the SAA's spatial structure and location — explaining why the anomaly exists beneath the South Atlantic and why it drifts westward at ~0.2–0.3°/year. The paper addresses the SAA's origin and geographic position, not the specific temporal rate of cell bifurcation (30.8→50.6° over 25 years). That bifurcation rate is consistent with westward-drift advection in MHD models, though the precise 0.8°/year figure is not specifically derived from the citation.
patches.rationale: The previous phrasing overstated what Terra-Nova 2017 demonstrates. The paper focuses on why the SAA exists (reversed flux at CMB) and why it occupies its observed location, not on modeling the temporal bifurcation rate. Our evidence should accurately characterize the citation to withstand scrutiny. The corrected phrasing keeps the core argument (MHD explains SAA origin) while being precise about what's cited vs inferred.

INTERMAGNET redesigned their website. The old /data-donnee/download-eng.php URL returns 404. The current data download page is at intermagnet.org/data_download.html, confirmed by INTERMAGNET's own site structure. Flagged by integrity check 2026-04-06T21:21.
